summary: McKenzie warns micro-ISV owners against misinterpreting website traffic data by failing to account for natural market cycles. Using his own Google organic traffic as an example, he shows how cyclical patterns like holiday breaks, weekends, and long weekends create noise that can be mistaken for the effects of algorithm changes or your own optimizations. He argues that before attributing any traffic change to a specific cause, you must first establish what the baseline pattern would look like without intervention. He illustrates this with his own recent meta description change, noting he doesn't yet have enough data to draw conclusions.
key_points: Traffic data contains natural cycles (weekends, holidays, school breaks) that can easily be mistaken for the impact of deliberate changes or external forces
Before measuring the effect of any change, you must understand what the data would have looked like without the change — establishing a proper baseline
Attributing traffic spikes to Google algorithm changes or your own tweaks without controlling for cyclical patterns leads to false conclusions
Even when you make a deliberate change, you need sufficient data over time before concluding it had a meaningful effect
money_quotes: One thing that is even more critical is that you understand what the data would have looked like had you not made the change, because otherwise you could be changing for a spurious reason.
What is actually happening here is that you are seeing the combination of natural cycles in my market combined with a sustained massive sustained increase in overall traffic since early December.
Resist the temptation — first, understand what the underlying pattern of your data should be absent any influence from you or the suspected outside force.
Its easy to get caught in the trap of saying that any momentary spike is either a reflection of your own action or of some outside force.
As of yet, focusing on just the last week of results, I do not have sufficient data to conclude that this change has produced a meaningful push in either direction.
key_insight: Before attributing any change in your metrics to a specific cause, you must first establish the baseline pattern your data follows naturally, or you risk optimizing based on noise.
